title: My First Year of Lean FIRE: The Real Experience
meta_title: My First Year of Lean FIRE: The Real Experience
subtitle: What the first 12 months of a small and free life abroad actually looked like
description: An honest first person account of my first year of Lean FIRE: leaving burnout in New York, landing in Da Nang, and building a free life on $1,100 to $1,800 a month.

faqs.question: What is the first year of Lean FIRE actually like?
faqs.answer: For me it was a year of rebuilding far more than relaxing. I spent it fixing my health with regular training, escaping a narrow tech bubble by meeting people from every walk of life, dating again with a clear head, and starting to build my own projects, all while living cheaply and freely in Da Nang.

faqs.question: Do you get bored after retiring early?
faqs.answer: You very easily can, and that is the real risk almost nobody warns you about. Sitting still forever would drive me up the wall, so I filled my days with training, community, travel, and building things like this site. The people who struggle most are the ones who plan the money perfectly and never once plan the time.

faqs.question: Is Lean FIRE lonely?
faqs.answer: It can be at the start, especially with the distance from old friends. Da Nang has a large and welcoming community of nomads and expats though, and board game nights, slow beach mornings, and regular meetups filled that gap far faster than I ever expected.

faqs.question: How much does your Lean FIRE life cost each month?
faqs.answer: My life in Da Nang runs on roughly $1,100 to $1,800 a month, which covers a nice apartment, great food, plenty of coffee, and regular travel. The move abroad is the single thing that shrank the number this dramatically.

faqs.question: Do you regret leaving your tech career?
faqs.answer: Not for a single day so far. I miss the salary far less than I ever expected, and I gained back my health, my time, and an actual personality in return. The hardest part was losing the professional identity, and even that slowly faded once I built a life that finally felt like mine.

cover_alt: Ninad on a coastal trip during his first year of Lean FIRE
